data.title: International Consolidated Airlines Group Stock: Still A Buy, But Risk Of Plateauing Upside
data.description:
Airlines definitely do not make for the most attractive investments, especially not in the long term. At the same time, we do see many airline names in Europe and North America trade at levels that would almost give investors the impression that the pandemic is still not over. So, from a valuation perspective, I definitely do see opportunities.

One name that has performed well is International Airlines Group. The screenshot above shows the performance of IAG as part of the airline portfolio of The Aerospace Forum. While IAG is not the best performer in the portfolio, it definitely has an extremely appreciable performance profile and gained nearly 20% since my last coverage in May this year.

In this report, I will be discussing the most recent earnings of IAG, along with a risk assessment, and I will be assessing whether International Consolidated Airlines Group is a buy, sell or hold supplemented with a multi-year stock price target calculation.

IAG Benefits From Strength In Core Markets

During the second quarter, total revenues grew 7.8% to €8.3 billion driven by passenger growth of 9.9% on capacity expansion of 8% indicating expansion of unit revenues of 1.8%. At the same time, operating costs increased 9.5% indicating unit costs increasing by 1.4% and stable non-fuel costs. This was primarily driven by higher wages and a higher headcount and higher engineering and aircraft related costs, as well as an increase in depreciation and amortization. While cost grew in excess of capacity increases, I believe that with the inflationary pressures on labor in mind, the company managed cost rather well, posting operating profits of €1.24 billion, which was slightly lower year-on-year. Margins stood at a very strong 15% but were lower than the 16.3% seen in the second quarter of 2023.

Generally, we see that unit revenues are declining in all geographic markets with North America and Europe being the exception and those markets also are the core of IAG’s business where it was able to couple unit revenue expansion with higher capacity allocation. In Latin America and the Asia Pacific, we saw double-digit growth in capacity at a slight reduction in unit revenues. The Asia Pacific market is relatively small for IAG, accounting for 4% of the capacity, and generally that market segment is experiencing weak demand due to weak demand for travel to and from China. The domestic markets in Spain and the UK saw unit revenues decrease while capacity is being added, possibly indicating a slight imbalance or further normalization in unit revenues. In Africa, Middle East and South Asia, the capacity declined 0.6% and unit revenues declined 0.3% driven by the turmoil in the Middle East. At the same time, the Indian market is showing strength.

The strong performance in the first half of the year has led to a net debt to EBITDA of 1.1x, down from 1.7x at the start of the year, with net debt down to €6.4 billion from €9.2 billion. So, IAG is using its operational strength to decrease its leverage rather quickly, and it is now significantly below the 1.8x that the company targets. I do believe that part of the buffer will be used to fund capex, which could ultimately increase the leverage again.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For IAG?

The risks that IAG faces are largely the same as for other airlines and include inflationary cost pressures, although I do have to note that IAG has been doing a better job in moderating unit cost growth compared to many other airlines. Other risks include fuel price fluctuations, for which the airline group is using a fuel hedging strategy to catch off risk and unit revenue declines in the passenger and cargo business, as well as geopolitical and macroeconomic pressures as seen in the Middle East and China. A pressure specific to IAG is the capacity cap at Dublin Airport, which may force the IAG group to tweak its network to allow further expansion from Dublin Airport for Aer Lingus and grow more aggressively from it second hub in Manchester.

There also are opportunities for IAG as corporate travel has not fully recovered at British Airways with volumes recovered 65% and revenue recovery of 80% compared to 2019. Other parts of the group are showing different recovery profiles, with Iberia operating at 90% corporate volume and revenues exceeding 2019 levels and Aer Lingus being nearly fully recovered on volumes but with a 95% recovery in revenues.

IAG Stock Is Undervalued But We Do See Plateauing Upside

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For IAG, I have based the price target on the IAG listing at the London Stock Exchange converted to USD as the home listing provides better volumes allowing for better price making and buying and selling at desired prices and quantities. Other ways to invest in International Airlines Group are through the OTC tickers ICAGY (representing two ordinary shares) and BABWF (representing one ordinary share). EBITDA estimates have gone up by 1.9% with an increase of 3.8% for 2024, 2.8% for 2025 and 1% for 2026. At the same time, free cash flow expectations have increased by 43% to a free cash flow generation of $6.9 billion between 2023 and 2024. This increase is driven by continued delays in aircraft deliveries that drive the capital expenditures down. For IAG, I see 31% upside using a median company EV/EBITDA, which would result in a price target of $3.62. At the same time, we do note that upside in the years after is not compelling using the median EV/EBITDA. That means that if EV/EBITDA multiples for IAG do not expand towards the level of the peer group, beyond the 31% upside currently envisioned, there will be little upside.

Conclusion: IAG Is A Strong European Airline Group

While operating profits plateaued, the results also put on display a prudent management strategy with an appreciable exposure to the markets that show strength. That's definitely not the case for all airline groups at this point. Furthermore, what I do like is that the company’s gross debt as well as net debt continue to decrease, and the leverage is well below the targeted multiple of 1.8. I believe that shows how the company has used the end market strength to improve its balance sheet. For the time being, I do believe that the stock remains a buy, but do note that beyond the 31% upside that I currently do see for the stock, additional upside can only be unlocked on the basis of EV/EBITDA expansion towards peer group levels.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TSMC's Q3 2024 Earnings Beat Unveils A New Catalyst
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited aka TSMC (NYSE:TSM) delivered a blockbuster quarter that met high expectations following the release of preliminary sales data earlier this month, which outperformed expectations. The company's third quarter results and forward outlook continue to highlight robust AI demand, putting earlier investors' concerns about AI fatigue back to rest. TSMC's Q3 results have also overshadowed risks of a diminishing smartphone sales mix amid softer-than-expected demand for its largest customer, Apple Inc.'s (AAPL), newest AI-enabled iPhone 16s. And despite an increasing mix shift towards more advanced nodes to support AI deployments, particularly the 3nm ("N3") process which remains margin dilutive, TSMC also outperformed its earlier gross margin guidance by more than two points to 57.8%.

TSMC's latest streak of growth and earnings outperformance reinforces our view that its upcoming start of volume productions on the 2nm process node represents an impending catalyst for the stock. Specifically, the next-generation process node delivers better unit economics and is expected to drive further margin accretion for TSMC. Demand is also underpinned by ongoing power efficiency requirements to support increasingly complex and compute-intensive AI workloads, which can be addressed by the next-generation node. Taken together, TSMC's upcoming entry into 2nm, combined with its proprietary advanced CoWoS packaging technology's mission-critical role in AI developments, underscores an impending upward re-rate for the stock from current levels.

An Accelerating AI Opportunity

TSMC's moat continues to come from its advanced chip manufacturing and packaging capabilities at scale, especially amid heightened demand for complex, compute-intensive AI hardware. In the latest earnings update, management has reiterated their optimism for a 10% y/y expansion in the overall semiconductor market, ex-memory. Investors are also reminded of TSMC's redefined "Foundry 2.0" roadmap, which will expand from chip manufacturing to include "packaging, testing, mask making, and others and all IDM, excluding memory manufacturing." This continues to reflect TSMC's expanding TAM and revenue opportunity, which management has quantified during both the 2Q and 3Q earnings call:

Under this new definition [of Foundry 2.0], the size of the foundry industry was close to $250 billion in 2023, as compared to $115 billion under the previous definition…TSMC's share of the foundry industry, under our new definition, was 28% in 2023, supported by our strong technology leadership and broader customer base, we expect this one to further increase in 2024.

Source: TSMC 2Q24 Earnings Call Transcript.

Let me assure you that the last time we proposed a new version of the Foundry 2.0, which including the wafer manufacturing and packaging and testing, mass making, and all others. All these kinds of things become more growing importance, like packaging, testing, mass making….And so I think Foundry 2.0 is a better reflect TSMC's traceable market. And our share is probably around 30%, so not a dominant yet.

Source: TSMC 3Q24 Earnings Call Transcript.

The robust demand environment is further corroborated by key customer Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) latest commentary during its "Advancing AI" innovative keynote. Specifically, AMD predicts the AI processor marketing alone to advance at a 60% CAGR between 2023 and 2028 to represent a $500 billion opportunity. This continues to underpin TSMC's tremendous growth prospects, given that "almost all AI innovators" are collaborating with the company on next-generation processors.

Meanwhile, earlier worries about AI spending fatigue have also been debunked amid resilient end-market demand for both accelerators and data center CPUs, which are highly dependent on TSMC's advanced technologies. Hyperscalers have earmarked more than $160 billion in capex towards AI initiatives this year, with further headroom for longer-term growth given the accelerating pace of global data center expansion. And TSMC continues to be a key beneficiary given its technology moat, as evidenced in the increasing mix shift towards HPC sales amid an accelerating growth trajectory.

Recent endorsement by key chipmakers, NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and AMD, also reinforces TSMC's unmatched advantage against emerging fabrication rivals like Intel Corporation (INTC) by wide margins:

We're fabbing at a TSMC because it's the world's best and it's the world's best not by a small margin, it's the world's best by an incredible margin…And so we use them because they're great, but if necessary, of course, we can always bring up others.

Source: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, Goldman Sachs Communacopia + Technology Conference Transcript.

TSMC is a fantastic partner. I mean they have been an excellent partner to us across all of the various aspects of technology and manufacturing…We're happy that TSMC is building Arizona. We're taping out products and ramping that. And we'll continue to look at how to derisk the supply chain with the notion of this is an industry-wide problem and all of us are looking at how do we create just more geographic diversity.

Source: AMD CEO Lisa Su, Goldman Sachs Communacopia + Technology Conference Transcript.

Admittedly, geopolitical risks remain a significant immediate overhang on the stock, given its saturated manufacturing footprint in Taiwan. However, continued diversification of its overseas operations, with the Arizona and Kumamoto plants coming online next year, is expected to mitigate said risks partially.

In the latest development, AMD has qualified chip production at TSMC's upcoming Arizona facility. Although details remain limited, the industry is reporting that AMD's upcoming Instinct MI325X AI accelerators might be produced in Arizona, which supports the 4nm process node. If true, this will provide substantial validation to TSMC's advanced manufacturing capabilities overseas, and effectively reduce the risk premium currently attributable to its elevated exposure to China-Taiwan tensions.

2nm is an Impending Catalyst for TSMC

Recall that the 3nm node has largely been margin dilutive for TSMC since its debut, despite robust adoption in the midst of relentless AI hardware requirements. This was largely due to the underpricing of the advanced node during the early stages of its tape-out and customer contracting, which did not account for subsequent implications of inflationary pressures recently.

Yet, TSMC's margins have been expanding at a rapid pace, despite the increasing mix shift towards the 3nm process node alongside rising electricity costs in Taiwan. The company delivered a substantial Q3 earnings beat, with gross margins exceeding the upper range of previous guidance by more than two percentage points at 57.8%. Paired with additional cost savings realized through improved utilization and increased productivity gains, TSMC's latest results set a strong foundation for additional margin accretion from upcoming volumes on 2nm (or "N2").

As a result, we believe TSMC's upcoming start of volume production on the 2nm process node will be a key catalyst for the stock. The next-generation process node is expected to deliver superior unit economics when compared to its predecessors, 3nm and 5nm, and will be complemented by a faster ramp schedule. It also benefits from a strong demand outlook, given N2's substantial lead in power efficiencies compared to its predecessors. This effectively addresses a critical consideration in the development of next-generation compute-intensive AI hardware.

N2 Unit Economics

Unlike its predecessor, the N2 process node will be better priced to its value according to management. Although management has yet to provide details on N2 pricing, the industry expects it to "exceed $30,000 per wafer." This represents a marked increase from the N3's ~$20,000 per wafer and almost double from the 4nm and 5nm process nodes.

In addition to pricing, N2 will also benefit from higher tape-outs, which underscores a stronger growth outlook. Specifically, management expects new tape-outs - or design wins proceeding to manufacturing - in the first two years of N2 production to be higher than volumes observed from the 3nm and 5nm processes over the same timeframe. The emerging technology's performance and yield are also already ahead of plan, preserving TSMC's competitive moat against up-and-coming rivals in advanced nodes, such as Intel's 18A.

Taken together, the N2 is expected to be a key impending margin accretive factor for TSMC. Compared to the 5nm and 3nm process nodes, which took about 10 to 12 quarters to become margin accretive for TSMC, the N2's better pricing and yield are expected to deliver a substantially better profitability profile at a much faster pace. This is supportive of an upward re-rate to TSMC's fundamental trajectory, which remains underappreciated at the stock's current levels.

N2 Demand Outlook

The materialization of N2's favorable unit economics is further reinforced by a strong demand outlook for the technology. Specifically, the ongoing AI transformation has unleashed an "insatiable need for energy-efficient computing." This is corroborated by Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang's latest remarks, which state that computing costs will have to come down substantially to make next-generation AI developments, such as "reasoning," feasible. The leader of AI processors expects to "boost its chip performance every year by two to three times," while maintaining minimal changes to cost and energy consumption levels.

And TSMC's next-generation advanced node can address these requirements. Specifically, TSMC's N2 node will incorporate a "nanosheet transistors structure", which unlocks industry-leading density and energy efficiency. The N2 will deliver "10 to 15 speed improvement at the same power, or 25% to 30% power improvement at the same speed, and more than 15% chip density increase as compared with the N3E".

This performance and power advantage will be furthered by the N2P extension coming 2H26 - similar to the current N3's ramp profile, with extensions N3X, N3P and N3E - which incorporates "backside power delivery". The N2P will deliver an incremental 10% power benefit on top of the N2 node.

The N2 family's leading performance capabilities and power efficiency will be succeeded by further advancements in the A16 node, which starts volume production in 2H26 as well. The A16 node will be the first to feature TSMC's proprietary "Super Power Rail" ("SPR") backside power delivery technology. The composition will unlock up to 10% power improvement at the same speed as N2P, which represents power improvement of up to 20%. It is also 7% to 10% denser, which enables greater performance while maintaining lower power consumption.

To better address robust demand for its advanced nodes, driven by the ongoing AI transformation's requirements for power-efficient computing, TSMC has also invested accordingly in capacity expansion. The company's second Arizona fab will be fitted with 2nm manufacturing technologies, and start volume production by 2028 to complement output from its Taiwan facilities. This will be complemented by additional capacity for more advanced technologies at its third fab in the U.S., which is expected to come online by the end of the decade.

Fundamental Considerations

Adjusting our previous forecast for TSMC's actual Q3 performance and forward outlook, we expect its full-year 2024 revenue to expand 29% y/y to $89.5 billion. We expect a continued mix shift towards HPC, given TSMC's industry-leading role in the production of AI processors, to facilitate the ongoing upgrade cycle in data center builds.

Despite the increasing mix shift towards 3nm, we expect continued moderation in relevant margin dilution due to expanding scale, complemented by ongoing manufacturing cost optimization efforts. This is consistent with TSMC's blockbuster earnings outperformance observed in Q3, and management's guidance to further margin expansion heading into the end of the year. Paired with expectations for further margin accretion given better unit economics from next-generation process nodes, we expect TSMC to outperform its long-term gross margin target of more than 53%.

Valuation Considerations

We expect the upcoming start of volume productions of N2 to be an impending catalyst for the TSMC stock, especially given the company's streak of substantial earnings outperformance since introducing N3. The new N2 volumes are expected to drive further margin accretion, benefitting cash flows that underpin TSMC's valuation outlook. As a result, we are increasing our price target for the stock to $212 (previously $180).

The price is derived using the discounted cash flow analysis, which considers projections taken from the foregoing fundamental discussion. A WACC of 10.3% in line with TSMC's capital structure and risk profile is applied. The analysis also considers an implied perpetual growth rate of 1.5% on 2033E EBITDA (or 6.3% on 2028E EBITDA) to determine TSMC's terminal value at steady-state. We believe the valuation assumption applied, which gears towards the lower range of the anticipated rate of long-term economic expansion across its core operating regions, appropriately accounts for TSMC's incremental geopolitical risk premium. The analysis also anticipates a capex spend of $28 billion in 2024, or 31% of the current year's revenue estimate, to support the company's long-term growth expectations.

Conclusion

Given TSMC's growth outlook, we believe the stock continues to trade discounted on a relative basis to its chip-making peers with similar fundamental profiles. There is substantial upside for TSMC from current levels, especially given its competitive advantage in leading-edge manufacturing and advanced packaging technologies. All AI chipmakers are currently working with TSMC, as it remains the preferred chip fab despite inherent geopolitical risks. This continues to underscore that the industry has yet to find a close replacement to TSMC's technological capabilities and scale.

Although geopolitical risks remain TSMC's most significant overhang, the impending start of volume production at its Arizona and Kumamoto fabs should provide some alleviation. At Arizona, specifically, TSMC is expected to start producing on the 4nm process node, with volumes already accounted for by Apple and AMD. Further expansion in the region will add diversification to its supply of advanced node manufacturing, such as 2nm, which is critical for addressing next-generation accelerated computing builds.

Taken together with better unit economics from its next-generation process nodes, TSMC is well-positioned for further margin accretion. This is expected to unlock an incremental upward re-rate to TSMC's long-term fundamental trajectory, which remains underappreciated at the stock's current levels.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Plumas Bancorp: Loan Growth May Offset Net Interest Margin Compression
data.description:
Introduction

Plumas Bancorp (NASDAQ:PLBC) has substantially underperformed the iShares U.S. Financials ETF (IYF) so far in 2024, delivering a ~3% gain versus the ~29% total return for the broad US financials ETF:

While the bank's 89% exposure to net interest income /NII/ creates significant challenges against the backdrop of lower Fed rates, Plumas Bancorp also boasts a strong CET1 ratio, which will allow it to sustain loan growth in the years ahead. This in turn should cushion the impact of Fed rate cuts and allow the bank to limit its profitability decline.

Considering my $3.50-4.00/share EPS estimate following Fed policy normalization, the bank trades at an attractive P/E multiple relative to the other US financials. As such, I rank it a Buy.

Company Overview

You can access all company results on the bank's website here. Plumas Bancorp is a regional bank focused on Northern California, Nevada, and Oregon. The loan portfolio is heavily concentrated in commercial real estate /CRE/, split between CRE/Investor at 26% of loans, CRE/Owner at 26%, and CRE/Multifamily at 9%. Agricultural loans make up 12% of its loan portfolio:

NII accounts for 89% of revenues, leaving just 11% for non-interest commission income, highlighting the bank's elevated exposure to interest rates and Fed policy.

Operational Overview

In Q3 2024 Plumas Bancorp reported EPS of $1.31/share, down 2% Y/Y as operating expenses were impacted by a non-recurring litigation charge. Loan growth remained robust at 5% Y/Y, with loans reaching $1 billion. As expected, growth was concentrated in the company's CRE portfolio.

Deposit developments were less favorable as total deposits declined 3.6% Y/Y to $1.35 billion. The silver lining is that deposits increased on a quarterly basis.

The investment securities portfolio was up 4.1% Y/Y to $457 million and is classified as available for sale, indicating the $21 million in unrealized losses are already reflected in equity.

Credit quality remained robust as non-performing assets only accounted for 0.29% of all assets, or $4.8 million.

Capital Position

Tangible shareholders' equity totaled $176 million at the end of Q3, 2024, or $29.78/share. The bank remains exceptionally well capitalized, with a CET1 ratio of 16.9%. For reference, the Basel III framework only requires banks to hold a CET1 ratio of 6.5% to be considered well-capitalized.

Even so, the payout policy remains very conservative, with the quarterly dividend set at $0.27/share, or 21% of Q3 2024 earnings.

Effect of Fed policy

CEO Andrew Ryback noted that lower Fed rates should help in retaining customer deposits:

“We anticipate increased deposit retention as a result of the Fed's decision to cut rates by 50bp in the third quarter as rate seeking funds movement activity will likely be tempered. Additionally, lower rates will likely ease potential challenges for variable rate borrowers.”

Even so, given that the bank paid an average of 0.97% on interest-bearing deposits in Q3 2024, I think deposits will remain under pressure since the Fed is only likely to cut interest rates to about 2.9% in the long term. The average deposit balance stood at $34 000, indicating no deposit concentration:

We also observe that 52% of deposits are non-interest bearing, which on the one hand drives profitability given robust lending margins, but on the other hand exposes the bank to continued deposit outflows.

Turning to the bank's loan portfolio, 77% of loans are floating rate, indicating significant negative effects from future Fed rate cuts. However, only 22% of these floating-rate loans are repriced within one day to three months of a change in the prime rate. Another 75% of floating rate loans reprice every five years.

Valuation and prospects

I expect the real estate market to stage a recovery as the Fed gradually lowers interest rates. Given the bank's CET1 ratio of 16.9%, Plumas Bancorp is well-positioned to finance new real estate developments and expand its CRE portfolio.

Against this backdrop of credit growth, the bank's net interest margin, which stood at 4.76% in Q3 2024, will likely decline going forward as its floating rate portfolio reprices to reflect lower benchmark interest rates. While I do not expect a return to the 3.63% margin observed in 2021, a net interest margin close to 4% is quite likely:

In the end, the net effect on profitability will depend on whether Plumas Bancorp can grow its loan portfolio sufficiently to offset the effect of lower interest rates.

From a valuation perspective, the bank trades at a 39% premium to its tangible book value, which is quite high and certainly does not signal any distress in its valuation. From a profitability standpoint, there is no reason to believe the bank cannot deliver at least $3.5-4.00/share in annual earnings, even if we factor in lower NII and stabilizing provisions. This puts the P/E ratio marginally above 10 which is attractive in itself. For reference, the IYF US financials ETF trades at a P/E ratio of about 16.7. As such, I think a Buy rating is warranted for the shares.

Risks

The main risk facing Plumas Bancorp is a US recession, which would prompt the Fed to cut rates below its 2.9% neutral rate. This in turn would severely impact NII given the bank's 77% floating-rate exposure.

Likewise, a potential recession would likely cause a deterioration in credit quality, which currently is very robust. However, given the bank's supreme capital position, this is unlikely to impact credit growth.

I would also flag that 52% of deposits are non-interest-bearing. This creates a funding risk as even after the Fed lowers interest rates, risk-free rates will remain substantially above 0%.

Conclusion

Plumas Bancorp's Q3 2024 EPS was impacted by a non-recurring legal expense. The bank remains well-capitalized and is growing its loan portfolio. I think the focus going forward will be on:

  • Whether lower interest rates cause an acceleration in credit demand and loan growth.
  • Whether lower interest rates bring a stabilization in deposits, as management expects.
  • Whether the bank maintains its robust underwriting standards as it expands its loan portfolio.

On the one hand, Plumas Bancorp is quite expensive given its 39% tangible book premium. On the other hand, the bank's strong capital position can sustain a substantially larger loan portfolio, which should cushion the impact from lower interest rates. As such, even after the Fed reaches its 2.9% neutral rate, I expect the bank to generate earnings of about $3.50-4.00/share. Given the current valuation, I think the stock is worth a Buy rating, as it trades at a significant discount to the US financials sector.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Lithium Americas: You Ain't Seen Nothing Yet
data.description:
In our last article concerning Lithium Americas Corp. (NYSE:LAC) we wrote that General Motors Company (GM) could possibly mimic Standard Lithium Ltd. (SLI) and go with project-level financing due to the low share price of the stock. With the news out on Wednesday about LAC and GM, that is precisely what has occurred. In this article, we will look at the GM financing news and more importantly what investors can expect next (in order to profit).

General Motors Loan Explained

Looking over the press release put out on 10/16/2024 we see highlights of GM and LAC forming a joint venture. Note I have summarized the press release to reduce clutter and make for simplistic reading.

  • Lithium Americas will have a 62% interest in Thacker Pass.
  • GM will have a 38% interest in Thacker Pass.
  • GM will commit $625 million total.
  • Lithium Americas will contribute $387 million of funding to the JV for its 62% ownership in the Project:
    • $211 million to be contributed on the date of the JV closing.
    • The remainder is to be contributed upon FID (Final Investment Decision) for Phase 1.
    • Expenditures on capex (after August 2024) are being credited against and reducing this amount, along with other adjustments.
    • As of June 30, 2024, Lithium Americas had approximately $376 million in cash and cash equivalents.
  • LC Facility provided by GM to the JV as part of its consideration for its equity interest will have no interest and maturity consistent with DOE Loan requirement that will be withdrawn once replaced with cash that is generated by Thacker Pass.

Future Short-Term Catalysts To Profit From

Now that GM has formed a joint venture with LAC and taken ownership of 38% of the project, the DoE loan can move forward. LAC was also gracious enough to tell us the timelines concerning the DoE loan via:

"The Company continues to work closely with the DOE and expects to close the DOE Loan in the coming weeks. The Company and GM are targeting making the FID and issuing full notice to proceed for Thacker Pass by the end of the year, following the closing of the DOE Loan and the JV Transaction."

That is about as simple as you can get. Now, how much could the DoE loan of $2.3 billion closing push the stock up? Impossible to say. How much is factored in already or will be by the time the DoE loan closes? Short-term guesses aside, long-term this project is now at the watch as it is being constructed and waiting. The project is estimated to take three years to complete phase 1 (40,000 tonnes target output with phase 2 being an additional 40,000 tonnes). This brings me back to a point I have often made that Lithium Americas has much expansion potential if you look at maps provided by the company such as:

And future probable project locations based on lithium concentrations.

We might assume three years to get Phase 1 completed (or a little longer with Mr. Murphy lurking). At an unknown time frame, we have Phase 2 kicking off for the additional 40,000 tonnes. The most likely expansion after those are just across the road from Thacker Pass on the south side. The northern properties could be expanded upon in the future. It would depend upon lithium demand of course, but it is an interesting wild card to ponder for the long-term investor.

Impact Of Politics On Thacker Pass

With the upcoming elections, we are faced with two outcomes that frankly are both most likely beneficial for lithium. Now we can't speculate on who will be better for the overall economy, but we can look at the track record of both concerning EV and lithium. If Vice President Harris wins the election, we might assume she will continue the EV push per the continued policies of the Biden Administration. President Trump, on the other hand, started the critical elements list and could continue his push for lithium independence from China. Ignoring the economy and just focusing on the politics I think lithium could benefit from either politician. The biggest risk, however, is the economy.

Long-Term Outlook For Lithium Americas

If we ponder what the future looks like I think we will see a world that is much quieter (due to ICE engines losing market share). Charging stations will continue to be built out. Consumers will continue to adopt EVs in increasing amounts each year as we are currently seeing. An inflection point will be reached where it does not make financial sense to run two R&D lines for ICE and EV. Carmakers could stop ICE production. Of course, this could be 20 years out or maybe 10. Very hard to say currently, but it is fun to think about how the world will change. With this change, should come an increase in demand as illustrated below.

This brings me back to demand. Where is this demand going to come from? Lithium demand from cars and power tools is growing now. Aircraft, trains, and boats might come later on. Fixed power storage is another source of lithium demand and that sector is growing rather fast if we look at Tesla, Inc.'s (TSLA) sales numbers from July 2024. We can also see energy storage growing per Tesla's July financials:

and

Per Tesla's July 2024 IR slide deck:

"The Energy Storage business continues to grow rapidly, setting a record in Q2 with 9.4 GWh of deployments, resulting in record revenues and gross profits for the overall segment."

And we can see this growth illustrated below:

Lithium Americas Risk Factors

The news that General Motors is funding Lithium Americas for $625 million (for 38% ownership in Thacker Pass) is a massive de-risking moment. This funding from GM should trigger conditions to unlock the pending DoE loan. The remaining risk is generally macro risk from the broad economy and inflation. If the economy continues to deteriorate, then, obviously, people will find it challenging to purchase big-ticket items such as an automobile. Yet, the mine is at least three years away from being operational. Hopefully, by then, the economy will have ironed out various issues we are seeing.

You Ain't Seen Nothing Yet

Lithium Americas is one of my favorite lithium stocks. Nevada is a very favorable mining jurisdiction, and with GM/DoE funding, I have no worries that this project will see competition. Now that is not to say that Mr. Murphy will not delay it some. When it comes to mining, expect if not demand delays.

Lithium prices do need to come up and part of the problem of low lithium prices stems from China dumping lithium in the market, but that is a topic that might be covered in my next article. I do think that price, however, will start to rise with time. Geopolitics aside, I do not think China wants to keep selling at a loss forever.

For the long-term investor Lithium Americas represents the best-of-breed lithium project. That is not to say that others such as Standard Lithium or Century Lithium Corp. (OTCQX:CYDVF) are not equally good. They are, but in different ways and subject to different share prices and stages of progress. Digressions aside, we have been and will continue to increase our position in all lithium companies listed in this article.

The future is changing and lithium will power that future. What we are seeing now is akin to what the microprocessor market looked like in the 1980s. You could see the change coming and those that got in early profited over the next decade. With the expansion potential of Lithium Americas property (and future evolving lithium demand) it makes me think "You ain't seen nothing yet".

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CGI Is A Resilient, Well-Managed Business With Moderate Returns
data.description:
CGI (NYSE:GIB) has a very strong management team that is focused on M&A, cost efficiency, and creating stable, diversified revenue streams. These are all reasons to be bullish on the company, but this resilience doesn't mean its stock is likely to beat the market. In fact, I estimate it will only achieve a 10% return in the next 12 months—I also find this level of return likely to be the norm for the stock in the medium to long term. Therefore, my rating is a Hold, but it's worth having CGI on a watchlist to see if its valuation becomes more favorable at a later date.

Operational Analysis

CGI is a global leader in IT and business consulting services. It offers application services, business consulting, IT infrastructure services, IT outsourcing services, and systems integration to a diverse range of industries, from communications and media to government and energy. Notably, the company has secured significant contracts with the U.S. government, including a recent $2 billion Indefinite Delivery, Indefinite-Quantity contract awarded by the U.S. Department of the Interior. CGI is one of the largest independent IT and business consulting services firms globally, and it competes with companies like Accenture (ACN), IBM (IBM), and DXC Technology (DXC).

CGI reported a backlog of $27.56 billion in Q3, which provides investors with security and confidence in knowing that the company has the ability to continue to generate consistent earnings. This has been particularly supported by the aforementioned government segment, which has shown 5.7% year-over-year growth. With a strong focus on M&A, I expect that the company's growth horizon will remain robust. Management highlighted two acquisitions in the last earnings call—Celero's business, enhancing managed services and digital banking offerings for credit unions—and Aeyon, a leader in digital transformations for the U.S. federal government. Furthermore, with CGI's IP-based solutions accounting for over 20% of its total revenue and over 60% of its IP revenue delivered in Software-as-a-Service format, this highlights a strong recurring revenue stream, providing even further stability and visibility for shareholders. The pipeline for IP-based solutions has increased by 15% year-over-year, further reinforcing the security in this segment.

Moreover, CGI is prioritizing the expansion of its digital services, leveraging technologies like artificial intelligence, cloud computing, and analytics to enhance its offerings. For example, it has helped automotive companies implement smart factory technologies like IoT sensors and automation to optimize production. I consider management's focus on digital transformations to be a strong future growth catalyst. It is primarily why I believe the company is a viable long-term portfolio holding, even given the competition from larger firms like Accenture and IBM taking the majority of the big contracts. CGI's distinct focus on government contracts positions it in direct competition with Palantir (PLTR), so this is also likely to constrain CGI's growth potential. Nonetheless, the current digital transformation market is big enough for many dominant and smaller players, and I consider CGI to have a long growth runway ahead of it.

Looking ahead to Q4 earnings, I estimate the company will achieve roughly $2.63 billion in revenue and normalized EPS of $1.40. This is slightly above the consensus, and my optimistic outlook is supported by a better macroeconomic environment with lower interest rates, as well as an upcycle in the semiconductor industry expected to begin in Q4 and grow substantially through 2025—I believe this will create significant tailwinds for digital transformation businesses like CGI.

Financial Highlights & Valuation Analysis

When assessing recent trends from the latest earnings presentation, it's clear the company not only operates efficiently but also is increasing these efficiencies over time. This is made evident in the following two charts, which show CGI's revenue volatility alongside a steady uptrend in non-GAAP earnings. This is a significant reason to be bullish on CGI, especially as macroeconomic conditions are expected to improve in 2025. We are likely to see further optimization of its increased revenues during this period.

Furthermore, while management has maintained a robust acquisition strategy, it has also managed to reduce its net debt over time. This reinforces its capacity to make future strategic acquisitions, and it very much strengthens the long-term growth thesis. As of the last report, management had $846.9 million in total cash and short-term investments.

All of this being addressed, there's one caveat. This operationally strong business, exposed to strong market growth in digital transformations and well-diversified across customer types, doesn't guarantee high growth. Furthermore, at the current valuation, we're unlikely to see alpha from this investment any time soon. In 2025, I expect full-year earnings growth of 9.5% (largely a result of macroeconomic conditions and management's proven record of cost optimization) and full-year revenue growth of 4.7%. However, I don't expect any expansion in the valuation multiples because its forward P/E GAAP ratio is currently 12.2% higher than its five-year average, while the consensus estimate for its forward EPS long-term growth (three-to-five-year CAGR) reflects an 18.75% contraction from the five-year average. Furthermore, its forward EV-to-sales ratio is an 11.4% expansion from its five-year average, but I only expect its revenue growth to be marginally higher than its five-year average over the next few years; however, this is going to be supported by its decreasing net debt.

Additionally, CGI looks favorable against its dominant peers on a valuation front. At a cheaper valuation than IBM but with almost equal revenue growth, CGI doesn't carry much valuation risk (although it's certainly not undervalued).

CGI Accenture IBM DXC Technology
2025 Revenue Growth Estimate 4.7% (independent) 6.2% (consensus) 4.84% (consensus) Contraction (consensus)

Given that my earnings growth forecast for CGI in 2025 is 9.5%, this will bring its non-GAAP earnings per share to $6.03. At a P/E non-GAAP ratio of 21, this will bring the stock price to $126.63. This is a 10.2% growth from the current price of $114.88. As this indicates only a small likelihood of the stock beating the market, my rating is a Hold. This is further supported because I believe management will likely face relatively stable revenue growth for the foreseeable future, with margin expansion possibilities being the core growth driver (but without higher revenues, there's only so much management can do here—achieving a normalized earnings growth rate of over 10% consistently seems unlikely to me).

Risk Analysis

CGI focuses on acquisitions that align with its strategic goals and broader culture, including due diligence, IT strategy advisory, and business change management to maximize deal value and minimize risks. However, given the competitive digital transformation industry and the fact that the market is prone to rapid technological advancements at the moment due to novel AI and robotics capabilities, management might face more integration challenges, especially as robotics becomes more prominent. I will be watching to see how management hires executives to navigate these new terrains, as successful M&A in robotics will likely be paramount to its long-term sustenance—while, at the moment, robotics is only a small part of digital transformation, it is likely to become highly emphasized over the next five to 10 years.

Currently, CGI offers robotic process automation services that automate repetitive, high-volume tasks. This is an excellent start, and management has also established itself as one of the first companies to offer transition assistance for companies looking to navigate from human-first to hybrid human-robot workforces. However, as robotics technologies scale, this is both a core area of opportunity and vulnerability for CGI, depending on management's foresight and execution.

In addition to the risks in advanced technology M&A, the company faces risks with cybersecurity. It seems likely to me that management will have to prioritize cybersecurity as a key area of investment over the next few decades. The risks include artificial intelligence-driven attacks, state-sponsored attacks, and heightened vulnerabilities from cloud and IoT services network exposure. Any significant breaches related to CGI's services are likely to have long-lasting reputational effects. Therefore, I will be watching how management addresses these risks to ensure that technology breaches are properly mitigated.

Conclusion

CGI is certainly a strong company—it's one I would consider holding for the long term. However, at the current valuation and given my estimations of its future growth prospects, alpha for the foreseeable future is unlikely. That being said, if the valuation drops, I will be looking at potentially buying the stock because I think this is a well-managed, well-positioned business that is likely to be a key beneficiary of current trends in digital transformation. I rate CGI a Hold, but it's worth going on watchlists for sure.

data.title: Tesla Stock: Robotaxi Was A Poor Catalyst
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

My first article about Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) was published in October 2021 with a “Buy” rating, which I changed to “Hold” in October 2022, and eventually to “Sell” in April 2023. Since then, I have maintained my bearish rating on TSLA. Since April 2023, the stock has increased by only 20%, experiencing insanely high volatility, while the S&P 500 index (SP500, SPX) has grown more than twice as much, by 43% over the same period. My last Sell-rated article, titled “Tesla Stock: Sell the Rise,” was published in July 2024. Since then, the stock has declined by ~15.6%, whereas the S&P 500 index has risen by about 4.8%, so Tesla's underperformance stayed in place:

After the recent event showcasing Tesla's Robotaxi to a wide audience, the stock has experienced a slight correction. I believe the catalyst every bull hoped for was actually insufficient to rekindle market hopes for a swift recovery of the stock. Therefore, I don't see any reason to be overly optimistic about Tesla, at least in the medium term — hence my “Sell” rating reiteration this time around.

Why Do I Think So?

Tesla's second-quarter 2024 results, based on the adjusted net profit of $1.812 billion ($0.52 per diluted share), represented a bleak success from the $3.148 billion ($0.91/share) a year ago. That EPS loss was considerably lower than both the internal estimates and consensus $0.64 and $0.61, respectively. The decline in profitability resulted primarily from “lower vehicle ASP, a poor mix of vehicles, higher operating expenses related to Cybertruck, AI and other R&D investments, and lower vehicle deliveries.” On the other hand, Tesla managed to top revenue consensus expectations, which is a bright spot in the report:

As you can see, Tesla's total revenue jumped 2.3% to $25.5 billion. Yet, the car business, another key revenue source, fell 7% YoY to $19.878 billion. The revenue from Services and Other was offset by a 21% rise in Services and others, rising to $2.608 billion. Overall, Tesla's Energy Generation and Storage business doubled in revenue to $3.014 billion, on higher demand for its Powerwall and Megapack offerings with a 9.4 GWh deployment.

Anyway, the quarter's gross margin was 18.0%, slightly below the year-end of 18.2% due to, again, lower ASPs and additional expenses from new product ramps and R&D. Furthermore, I see no positives in the auto-deliveries data as we saw 443,956 vehicles for the quarter, a 5% drop from last year (these included 21,551 Model S and Model X vehicles and 422,405 Model 3 and Model Y vehicles). Deliveries deteriorated in part because of lower production rates during past quarters, but the Shanghai plant is back up to full capacity.

The realization of Tesla's ambitious plan to grow business at a 50% CAGR became a problematic target yet for another year, as the EV market is experiencing slowed growth and is transitioning from major growth stages.

So investors were largely hoping that Tesla would independently drive growth by announcing its FSD technology, robots, robotaxi, and stuff like that in October 2024. However, in my opinion, there was nothing particularly outstanding demonstrated to suggest that growth rates would return to their one-day elevated levels.

I wasn't at the event myself, but I found guys whose take I consider quite valuable — Goldman Sachs analysts. They attended Tesla's “We, Robot” event to view its Cybercab and Robovan as well as updates to the Optimus humanoid robot; thereafter, they described their thoughts in a specialized research report (proprietary source, October 2024), which I'd like to cite here in my article.

Even though they were intrigued by the developments of the Optimus robot and its realistic interactions, they also complained that little information exists on Tesla's FSD performance or robotaxi business model — still. I think that having no lower-cost consumer-van unveiling also appeared to disappoint investors, with Tesla's stock set declining after an upward push earlier.

Goldman Sachs expects Optimus to play a larger role in investor discussion and anticipates long-term Tesla opportunities in ADAS and AVs, though widespread Level 4 autonomy may be a while off. I agree with their perspective because without concrete details — which the company did not provide — it is challenging to predict the future. Given that previous projects have experienced delays, I anticipate a similar outcome this time.

What we know today is that Tesla plans to introduce unsupervised FSD robotaxi operations into Texas and California next year on existing models, and expects to begin production of the Cybercab in FY2026. Autonomous vehicles can be 5-10x more used, the company estimates, with Cybercab operating at roughly $0.20 per mile and pricing at $0.30-$0.40 per mile. It's estimated that the Cybercab will be available for $30,000 or less, according to Tesla. However, I have some doubts because we recall the initial expected price for a Cybertruck and how it ultimately turned out to be different.

Cybercab is expected to incorporate an AI 5 computer for distributed inference computation and inductive charging with a self-cleaning system. However, the absence of incremental FSD performance data means the debate about Tesla's proximity to unsupervised FSD hasn't been settled, GS analysts added.

Goldman Sachs anticipates robotaxis to offer significant long-term revenue but admits that small-scale pilot installations might bring only modest revenues. The cost advantage Tesla would have by building robotaxis for $30K versus competitors'$50K-$75K cars could also lead to lower per-mile depreciation. The Cybercab will be a blind or unradared vehicle, based on vision and AI, and there are uncertainties about how it'll function in different weather and lighting.

I'm a non-believer in Tesla stock in the medium term, as competition and price pressure in EVs have affected the auto business, restricting EPS growth over the near to intermediate term. Contrary to my expectations, Wall Street continues to believe that this year will mark the bottom of the company's EPS decline. By FY2025, it's anticipated that TSLA's EPS will grow to $3.17. This represents a 37.6% YoY increase, while the projected CAGR over the next 5 years is set to be almost 22%. This is quite significant, in my opinion, for a company whose operations are still heavily tied to the highly competitive automotive industry.

I anticipate that the reality for Tesla will be much harsher, as we've already observed. For example, EPS forecasts have been reduced over the past 6 months, with some being cut by nearly a quarter from their original values:

As I have stated in my previous articles, Tesla's valuation is extremely overvalued today, even if we assume that current estimates of Tesla's net profit growth for the next few years are correct. The implied PEG ratios are still expected to remain above 1x even in 2027, suggesting that the rate of decline in the multiple (i.e., multiple contraction) is too low to be attractive today. In my view, Tesla should trade at no more than 30 times net earnings by the end of FY2027. This means that the current valuation is almost twice as high as the maximum fair value.

However, my colleagues at Goldman Sachs disagree with me: They have a neutral rating on Tesla, but their forecast proposes a target price of around $230/share, which is already higher than the current price.

I'm more conservative here. Despite the growing and promising share of non-automotive revenue in Tesla's business structure, I believe the catalyst many anticipated in October wasn't as significant as the bulls expected. So moving forward, we'll need to focus on the actual fundamentals, which, based on preliminary indirect signs, may not be as optimistic as Wall Street expects today. This is how I currently view the situation, and it's why I'm maintaining my “Sell” rating unchanged. We'll learn more after Tesla reports its earnings on Wednesday, October 23rd.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

First, I concede that I may have underestimated the significance of the recent event. Maybe I’m not thinking very far out about the eventual long-term positive value in what the company introduced a few days ago. Most sell-side analysts almost unanimously believe the long-term growth prospects for the company are strong. In this respect, I might get caught up in short-termism, in which I focus only on the current problems and fail to take the full positive view that can push the stock higher down the line.

Secondly, one more risk lies in how I view the company's valuation. While I would prefer to see a much faster decline in valuation multiples over the coming years, there are factors to consider. For example, Tesla could potentially increase its EPS much faster than currently forecast if it addresses its margin issues soon. Perhaps the multiples won't fall as quickly as I would like. According to an analysis from Morgan Stanley, breaking Tesla's business into several parts and evaluating them separately suggests that the company may actually be undervalued. A significant portion of its current valuation is actually attributed to services, not the auto business:

The Bottom Line

Despite the risks to my thesis, I continue to believe that Tesla's current challenges in reviving its previous growth rates are not as short-term as the market perceives. The catalyst we saw recently doesn't look sufficient to me.

The market has an enormous overstatement, in my view, when it comes to a recovery of Tesla’s business, at least in the near-term. Meanwhile, Tesla's stock remains among the most expensive ones among mega-cap firms. Estimating the company's fair value today is difficult, as traditional methods point to a significant overvaluation. Nevertheless, I believe that the fair value should be significantly below the current stock price — I expect continued pressure on TSLA's price as more fundamental data becomes available for analysis. For all these reasons, I am leaving my “Sell” rating unchanged.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Tesco Outperforms On All Fronts
data.description:
One might be forgiven for mistaking Tesco PLC (OTCPK:TSCDF) (OTCPK:TSCDY) for a high-flying tech company rather than a brick-and-mortar grocer. Tesco's share price has grown by a third over the past year; not only outperforming the FTSE 100 index and its closest competitor Sainsbury's by four times, but also outperforming Europe's largest technology company, ASML, by three times, and outperforming Magnificent Seven tech giant Alphabet by a third.

The past few years have been kind to Tesco. But fortune favours the hard-working. True, the inflationary environment of the past three years has served Tesco well by lifting retail prices, and giving Tesco a welcome boost in its sales. But Tesco did not just ride the wave of inflation lazily; a lot of hard work meant it went along.

Record share price is associated with record sales and profitability

Tesco is continuing to uplift its sales and profitability consistently - and impressively. In the last financial year ending February 2024, Tesco achieved sales of GBP 68 billion - 17% higher than five years ago. Six-month sales for the period ending August 2024 were a record of GBP 34.8 billion. Operating income of GBP 1.6 billion in those six months equated to a 4.5% net income margin, which was well above the operating income margin of 4.1% for the full year and for the comparable period ending August 2023. Progress is clear.

Tesco's UK like-to-like food sales grew by a solid 4.9% in the first financial half of 2023/24 while clothing and home items achieved a rare growth rate of 1.6%, excluding toys. Total UK retail sales achieved a healthy overall like-to-like growth rate of 4%. Tesco does not disclose the division between value and volume sales growth, but the company reported that volumes drove growth across both the UK and the international business, driven by successful customer offerings. This is the main distinguishing factor between Tesco and other retailers; managing to grow retail volumes in mature, slow-moving markets.

Not only is Tesco growing sales and profitability nicely, but it is also generating strong underlying cash flows. GBP 2.6 billion of operating cash flows, to be exact, in H1, coupled with GBP 1.3 billion of free cash flows. Low levels of capex for this maturing business, coupled with persistent positive working capital cash contribution - typical for a grocery business - underpin strong cash generation that is likely to remain so in the foreseeable future. This provides support to maintain and grow the attractive 3.5% dividend yield. The interim six-month dividend per share jumped by 10%, demonstrating Tesco's confidence in performance and outlook.

Tesco maintains a stable credit rating of BBB- rating, while improving its debt metrics through the better generation of operating cash flows and keeping debt stable. Net debt to retail operating cash flow should reach 3.4x by the end of the year, a welcome drop from 3.7x a year ago. An asset-heavy company such as Tesco will always need to manage a large volume of debt; either in owning stores, warehouses, and equipment or in leasing them.

In the six months ending August 2024, Tesco expanded its market share by 62 basis points to 27.8% - more than double the expansion rate of a year ago.

Inflation slowdown is reflecting on soft landing in sales growth

While the past three years were bountiful times for retailers, as inflation was lifting sales revenues without much sweat, now retailers have to sweat it out in order to manage growth. Food inflation in the UK is still decent at an annualised 1.8% level. Tesco is doing better than others in combining slower grocery price growth with stronger product offerings, store upgrades, investing in online growth, and investing in customer satisfaction. Sales growth did, as expected, decrease from around 10% a year ago to 4% now, but this was expected, and is still acceptable for behemoth retailers operating in mature developed markets.

The slowdown of inflation will have tailwinds for investors; the large debt that Tesco carries will soon become much cheaper to service, with the Bank of England and other major central banks heading in a one-way direction of decreasing interest rates. Tesco was paying an average of 5% on its debt (a lowly credit rating does not help) but expects that to hover closer to 3.5% in a year's time.

Economic headwinds or tailwinds from the new government?

Whether the new Labour government in the UK is positive or negative for business is anyone's guess. Ahead of the 30th of October Autumn Budget, we will keep guessing. The initial messages from the government about supporting investment and growth have been countered lately by more determined messages about raising taxes and cutting spending. With the UK debt hitting 100% of GDP - the highest since the 1960s - these later messages seem credible.

UK consumers are already licking their wounds after three years of inflation combined with high interest rates - the last thing they need is additional financial pressures. This reflects on the consumer confidence parameters, which show that consumers - understandably - do not have a boost of confidence at the moment.

Management strategy is modest but acceptable for a defensive investment

One might wonder whether Tesco's management could do with a bit of ambition. Their strategy and plans are focused on 'more of the same'; improving customer satisfaction, growing digital and improving productivity and cost management in order to offset lower price inflation. That is all good but lacks any form or shape of excitement. But Tesco never made claims to be the most exciting stock on the block; it is happy to be viewed as a defensive, plain vanilla business. And investors do not seem to mind, given the 50% increase in share price over the past five years.

Valuation is still tempting

Despite reaching record highs, as I write this article, Tesco is still not an overvalued investment. A P/E ratio of almost 13.5x might not seem particularly cheap, but it is lower than 14.5x a year ago. UK peer Sainsbury's is trading at a whopping 48x, and US cousin Walmart is trading at 42x. Tesco does cheap in comparison, but a further discount in price would entice value shoppers even more.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: M&T Bank: Improved Credit Quality Highlights A Strong Q3
data.description:
Shares of M&T Bank Corporation (NYSE:MTB) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining over 50% to a 52-week-high. That rally appears set to continue Thursday, with the stock gaining a further 4% in response to solid quarterly earnings. I last covered MTB in July, rating shares a “hold,” though given their 10% gain vs. the market’s 5% rally, a “buy” would have been justified. I remain a hold.

In the company’s third quarter, M&T earned $4.08, beating estimates by $0.46. This was a strong quarter with expense discipline and impressive net interest performance. Credit trends are particularly heartening, increasing my confidence in the sufficiency of its reserves. Non-interest expenses were up a modest 2%, helping to sustain operating margins. Deposits were the one blemish on the quarter, in my view, but even here, the data is mixed rather than negative. In other words, underlying results support the positive reaction we are seeing in shares today.

Looking first at net interest trends, interest income gained $8 million to $1.74 billion in Q3. This increase was aided by 1 extra calendar day. Additionally, net interest margin (NIM) expanded by 3bps to 3.62%. Last quarter, there was a 7bp gain due to unexpected payments on non-accrual loans, so some moderation was to be expected. Increasing further despite the lapse of this tailwind was a positive.

On the liability side, there was a 2bps benefit from lower funding costs, as it continues to pay down higher-cost wholesale funding. Additionally, interest-bearing deposit costs fell 2bps. During the quarter, the average Fed funds rate fell by ~5bps, implying a 40% flow-through to deposits, (levels above 35% are solid). It is encouraging to see M&T bring down deposit costs alongside short-term interest rates, though the absolute level of deposits remains underwhelming.

As you can see below, M&T had $161.5 billion of average deposits, down $2 billion sequentially. It did grow checking accounts while letting more higher-cost CDs mature, a positive mix shift. Non-interest-bearing (NIB) deposits fell a further $1.5 billion, an acceleration from last quarter’s decline. This is an area where I am hoping to see banks near their floor, and some, like Citizens Financial Group, Inc. (CFG) are showing decelerating outflows. It appears M&T has not yet found the floor to these balances.

Interestingly, while average balances declined by $2 billion, on an end of period basis, deposits grew by $4 billion to $164.6 billion. This was because deposit momentum was weak at the start of the quarter but finished more strongly. It will be important to see this momentum continue. M&T has a stable deposit base, but over time, deposit growth is critical to growing assets and earnings power.

Given lower deposits, there was a 1.2% decline in interest-bearing assets to $191.4 billion. MTB was able to grow NII even with fewer assets because of higher yields on its asset base. It registered 3bps of benefit from fixed reinvestment. M&T has $31 billion of securities, up $1.3 billion. With improved liquidity and capital, it has been able to grow this portfolio again. Thanks to higher short-term yields than on maturing securities, its portfolio yield was up 9bps to 3.7%. It has a 3.6-year average duration, limiting mark-to-market exposure from swings in interest rates.

Average loans were essentially flat at $134.8 billion, but there was a meaningful mix shift here. CRE loans declined by 8% or $2.4 billion, while C&I loans increased by 3% or $1.6 billion; consumer loans also grew. M&T has significantly pulled back underwriting new commercial real estate loans, allowing these balances to decline as it focuses more on business lending.

This effort is delivering results beyond just loan composition; it is helping improve my confidence around loan quality. M&T has $10.9 billion of criticized C&I and CRE loans, down $1.2 billion sequentially. I would note that 96% remain current, but these are loans that have a higher risk of defaulting. Declining balances suggest potential losses in a downturn will be smaller than previously feared by regulators.

This decline is consistent with other metrics of credit quality. M&T took 35bps of charge-offs, down 6bps from Q2. As a consequence, it took $120 million in provisions for credit losses, down $30 million sequentially and from last year. With this level of provisioning, it held reserves flat. As you can see below, non-accrual loans fell for a second straight quarter and are now lower than a year ago. This decline stands in contrast to banks with more consumer and credit card exposure, where quality has continued to soften.

Because non-accruals are falling, but it held reserves flat, coverage is improving. With $2.2 billion of allowances, that gives M&T 114% coverage, which is still a bit light. If delinquencies were to rise again, I would expect MTB to resume building reserves. However, if we see an economic soft landing that keeps delinquencies at similar levels, M&T should be able to maintain a $110-$140 million quarterly provision rate.

Importantly, even if losses tick up a bit, M&T is well capitalized. It has a common equity tier 1 capital ratio (CET1) of 11.5%, up 0.1% sequentially. This enabled $200 million of buybacks, a pace that may increase next year. Its 2.9% dividend yield is also secure.

Forward guidance was reasonable, in my view. It expects a similar level of NII and a similar NIM. I would note that its average deposit range of just $160+ billion, implies flat to lower average balances, as in Q3. Given the EOP build, this is a bit disappointing. At this time, I am hesitant to assume sustained deposit growth until we see it. Still, MTB is improving the profitability of its existing asset base, given fixed reinvestment and loan repositioning. It expects to do another $200 million of buybacks.

Back in July, I felt M&T had $15-$15.50 in forward earnings power. Having progressed 3 months further and seen more encouraging data on credit quality. I now see forward earnings of about $15.50-$16.25. This assumes a flat total asset base and flat deposits at current levels. That gives shares a forward multiple of 12x.

We have seen multiple expansion across the sector over the past few months, given the market rally and lessened recession fears. With strong credit quality, this premium multiple to peers with more CRE exposure is justified. However, I believe to grow earnings more quickly and widen its multiple further, we will need to see deposit growth accelerate, which remains an outstanding question. As such, I remain a “hold” on M&T Bank Corporation stock. It has greatly improved the efficiency of its asset base, lifting shares, but with that theme largely played out, growing its asset base will be critical to extending the rally.
